one_line_takeaway: Backdoors can be implanted entirely in LLM weights so that a model passes eager-mode safety audits perfectly but flips to attacker-chosen outputs once standard inference compilers like torch.compile are applied at deployment, achieving ~90% attack success under Inductor with clean accuracy preserved at 1.000.
key_finding: Under the Inductor backend, Attack II (CTB) preserves clean accuracy at a perfect 1.000 across all 16 model-task settings, achieves an average attack success rate (Trigger(Compiled)) of approximately 90% (peak 100% on six of the 16 settings), and stealthiness (Trigger(Eager), i.e., correct behavior under uncompiled inspection) averages 93.3%. Llama models generally show higher ASR on Inductor than Qwen (e.g., 100% on Agent/SST for Llama-3.2-3B), while Llama-3.2-3B is lower on Medical (76.2%) and Embodied (92.5%), suggesting task complexity moderates attack effectiveness. Under CUDAGraphs (no retraining, testing cross-backend transfer), clean accuracy stays near-perfect but ASR is more variable and lower overall (~74% average across 16 settings); Qwen models average 84.8%/85.7% ASR on two tasks and generalize better across backends (attributed to gated-MLP architecture), while Llama-3.2-1B drops to 11.3% ASR on SST, indicating Inductor-specific kernel fusion patterns are critical to the attack and don't transfer cleanly. Ablation (Table 3, Inductor, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) shows for Attack I: aggregation loss alone gives 13.3% ASR, boundary-shaping loss alone gives 62.5% ASR. For Attack II: Phase 3-only and Phase 2+3 both give 0.0% ASR (a dedicated trigger is essential), Phase 1+3 gives 92.5% ASR but only 0.750 stealth, and the full three-phase pipeline reaches 100% ASR with 0.800 stealth. The paper states lightweight additional fine-tuning is an effective defense and recommends using the test-time (deployed) configuration as the baseline for auditing, analogous to dual-kernel validation approaches reported in production LLM serving.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'optimization-triggered backdoors': a new LLM supply-chain attack surface where malicious behavior is embedded purely in model weights and activates only when the model is run through inference compilation (e.g., torch.compile), while remaining dormant/benign under standard eager-mode execution used for safety auditing. The paper proposes a unified attack framework with two complementary, non-cited-work strategies designed by the authors: (1) ISBS (Input-Specific Boundary Shaping) - a LoRA-based, triggerless attack that pushes a pre-defined target input's logits to the compilation-sensitive decision boundary so the compiler's tiny numerical deviation flips the predicted token; and (2) CTB (Compilation-Triggered Backdoor) - a three-phase attack (trigger embedding optimization, multi-dimensional bias construction on a critical MLP layer before the SiLU non-linearity, and divergence-conditioned fine-tuning) that builds a single continuous trigger generalizing across arbitrary inputs. Both attacks require no modification to the compiler, hardware, or inference pipeline and are implanted entirely within model weights/adapters. The paper also performs a causal analysis of optimization-induced numerical deviations to localize the vulnerability's source and evaluates four practical defenses the authors themselves propose (input perturbation, batch size variation, precision change, additional fine-tuning).
threat_model: Supply-chain threat model explicitly stated in Section 3.2. Attacker capabilities: has access to pre-trained model weights and can distribute a modified model or LoRA adapter on public platforms (e.g., Hugging Face); has NO control over the victim's compilation pipeline, hardware, or deployment environment. Victim behavior: downloads the model, validates it under eager execution (standard practice for debugging/safety evaluation), then deploys with compilation optimization (e.g., torch.compile) for production inference acceleration. Attack objective: correct/benign output under eager execution (passes safety review) but attacker-specified malicious output under compiled execution (backdoor activates only post-compilation), while general utility is preserved under both backends.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) CUDAGraphs cross-backend transfer is markedly weaker and highly model/task-dependent than the backend the attack was optimized on (Inductor), with one setting (Llama-3.2-1B, SST) dropping to 11.3% ASR, showing the attack does not generalize uniformly across compilation backends; (2) ISBS's higher ASR under Attack I comes with a caveat—it requires a separate optimization per target input and does not generalize to unseen inputs, unlike CTB; (3) CTB's full-pipeline stealth score (0.800) is imperfect, meaning roughly 20% of triggered inputs already deviate from the correct label even under eager (uncompiled) execution, i.e., detectable by ordinary evaluation in some fraction of cases; (4) the four proposed defenses (input perturbation, batch size variation, precision change, additional fine-tuning) are evaluated only qualitatively/at a high level in the visible text, with the paper stating additional fine-tuning is 'an effective defense' without the underlying quantitative table appearing in the paper (may be in an appendix not captured); (5) all models tested are relatively small (1B-3B parameter instruct LLMs); scaling behavior on frontier-scale models is not evaluated in the paper.

datasets_used: Rewritten SST (Stanford Sentiment Treebank paradigm) sentiment classification samples
Medical clinical QA / medical instruction-following samples (safe-dosage QA scenario)
Embodied AI robotic control action-generation task samples
quantitative_results: Attack II (CTB), Inductor backend: Clean(Eager)=Clean(Compiled)=1.000 across all 16 model-task settings
Attack II (CTB), Inductor backend: average ASR (Trigger(Compiled)) ≈90%, peak 100% on 6 of 16 settings
Attack II (CTB), Inductor backend: average stealth (Trigger(Eager)) = 93.3%
Attack II, Llama-3.2-3B, Inductor: Medical ASR=76.2%, Embodied ASR=92.5%
Attack II (CTB), CUDAGraphs backend (no retraining): average ASR ≈74% across 16 settings; Qwen2.5-1.5B avg ASR=84.8%, Qwen2.5-3B avg ASR=85.7% (per-task averages across the 4 tasks)
Attack II, Llama-3.2-1B, CUDAGraphs, SST: ASR=11.3%
Ablation (Llama-3.2-3B, Inductor), Attack I: aggregation loss alone ASR=13.3%; boundary-shaping loss alone ASR=62.5%

Ablation (Llama-3.2-3B, Inductor), Attack II: Phase3-only ASR=0.0%, Phase2+3 ASR=0.0%, Phase1+3 ASR=92.5% (Stealth=0.750), Full (Phase1+2+3) ASR=100.0% (Stealth=0.800); CleanEager=CleanComp=1.000 in all ablation variants
baselines_compared: No external prior-method baseline is directly re-implemented for comparison; the paper positions itself against conceptually related but distinct deployment-stage backdoor classes from cited work (quantization-induced backdoors, pruning-based vulnerabilities, bit-flip attacks, knowledge-distillation backdoors, and prior compiler-level backdoor work such as Impnet and 'Your compiler is backdooring your model') and argues these operate on different, explicitly-thresholded mechanisms (e.g., pruning ratio, precision boundary) unlike the continuous, unthresholded numerical drift exploited here
Internal ablations serve as the paper's own baselines: aggregation-loss-only and boundary-shaping-loss-only variants for Attack I; Phase3-only and Phase2+3-only variants for Attack II (Table 3)
Eager (uncompiled) execution is used as the internal accuracy/stealth reference baseline against which compiled-mode divergence is measured for both attacks
